message.content:
The air in Mr. Abernathy’s shop was thick with the scent of old paper, brass polish, and dust motes dancing in the single beam of afternoon sunlight. The walls were lined floor-to-ceiling with clocks of every shape and size—grandfather clocks with pendulums swinging like heavy hearts, cuckoo clocks with wooden birds, and delicate pocket watches ticking in unison, creating a low, rhythmic hum that felt less like noise and more like breathing.

Leo, seven years old and wearing a t-shirt stained with grape juice, stood on his tiptoes to peer over the counter. He held a small, broken toy train in one hand.

"Mr. Abernathy?" Leo whispered, as if speaking too loudly might shatter the silence.

The old man looked up from his workbench, where he was bent over a tiny, intricate mechanism with a pair of tweezers. His spectacles were perched precariously on the end of his nose. "Yes, Leo? And please, no shouting. The seconds are trying to rest."

Leo frowned, looking around at the hundreds of ticking faces. "But they aren’t resting. They’re all moving. Why do they have to move so fast? Can’t they just... stay still?"

Mr. Abernathy set down his tweezers and wiped his hands on a rag. He smiled, the lines around his eyes deepening. "Come here, Leo. Sit on this stool."

Leo climbed onto the wooden stool, his legs dangling.

"Do you know what time is?" Mr. Abernathy asked softly.

Leo thought for a moment. "It’s when I have to go to bed. Or when the ice cream truck comes."

Mr. Abernathy chuckled, a dry, rattling sound. "Those are *moments*, Leo. Time is
